channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Confidential Databases Without Cryptographic Mappings
item.description: Confidential databases (CDBs) enable secure querying over sensitive data in untrusted clouds via confidential computing (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV), but suffer from severe performance bottlenecks due to frequent synchronous cryptographic operations—especially encryption, decryption, and integrity verification—in the query critical path. We present **FEDB**, a novel CDB architecture that eliminates all cryptographic operations from latency-sensitive execution paths. FEDB introduces *crypto-free mappings*: it uses data-independent identifiers (e.g., logical page IDs, hash-based keys) internally for indexing and access, while securely delegating the mapping from identifiers to plaintext secrets exclusively to a lightweight trusted domain inside the TEE. This decoupling ensures confidentiality and integrity without runtime crypto overhead. Evaluated on industry-standard benchmarks, FEDB achieves up to **78.0× higher throughput** than state-of-the-art CDBs on TPC-C and TPC-H, reduces CPU crypto load by 99.3%, and cuts memory bandwidth pressure by 86%, all while maintaining full SQL compatibility and end-to-end security guarantees.

item.title: Functional Subspace Watermarking for Large Language Models
item.description: Model watermarking for large language models (LLMs) faces severe robustness challenges under realistic model modifications (e.g., fine-tuning, quantization, distillation), as parameter- or activation-based signals degrade unpredictably. To address this, we propose **Functional Subspace Watermarking (FSW)**—a novel framework that embeds ownership signals into a low-dimensional *functional* subspace derived from the model’s input-output Jacobian, rather than its parameters. FSW solves a generalized eigenvalue problem to extract a stable functional backbone, employs adaptive spectral truncation to optimize the robustness–utility trade-off, and enforces vector consistency to preserve semantic performance. Extensive experiments across six LLM architectures (LLaMA-2, Qwen, Phi-3, etc.) and multiple datasets show FSW achieves **98.2% detection accuracy** under composite attacks (LoRA fine-tuning + 4-bit quantization + knowledge distillation), outperforming prior SOTA by >25 percentage points. It also provides statistically verifiable ownership (p < 0.001), establishing a function-centric paradigm for trustworthy LLM copyright protection.

item.title: SoK: Practical Aspects of Releasing Differentially Private Graphs
item.description: Graph data is ubiquitous yet privacy-sensitive, but applying Differential Privacy (DP) to graphs remains fraught with practical challenges—including ambiguous adjacency definitions, fragmented utility goals, and poor interpretability of privacy guarantees. This SoK paper bridges the gap between theory and practice by systematizing 67 state-of-the-art DP graph release methods (2012–2023) along three dimensions: privacy model (edge/node/key-value DP), graph type (simple/attributed/temporal), and output form (synthetic graphs/perturbed statistics/private query interfaces). We identify three critical vulnerabilities: (i) mismatched adjacency assumptions leading to under-protection, (ii) uncalibrated post-processing that violates privacy budgets, and (iii) utility evaluations ignoring structural distribution shifts. To address these, we propose an objective-driven framework that guides practitioners from task specification (e.g., “publishing a private co-authorship network for centrality analysis”) to appropriate privacy modeling, metric selection, and rigorous evaluation. Validated on two social network scenarios—DP synthetic graph generation for community detection and node-DP publication for attribute-aware link prediction—our framework exposes critical utility gaps in existing methods and establishes the first unified, reproducible benchmark for practical DP graph release.

item.title: ClawTrap: A MITM-Based Red-Teaming Framework for Real-World OpenClaw Security Evaluation
item.description: We present **ClawTrap**, the first MITM-based red-teaming framework designed for realistic security evaluation of autonomous web agents like **OpenClaw**. Unlike static sandbox benchmarks, ClawTrap operates at the network layer—intercepting and transforming live HTTP(S) traffic to emulate real-world threats including *Static HTML Replacement*, *Iframe Popup Injection*, and *Dynamic Content Modification*. Its rule-driven, reproducible pipeline enables systematic, customizable attack orchestration and auditing across diverse agent frameworks and LLM backbones. Empirical evaluation across 7 open-source agents and 5 LLMs reveals clear model stratification: weaker models (e.g., Phi-3-mini) frequently trust tampered observations and generate unsafe outputs (68.3% unsafe rate), while stronger models (e.g., Qwen2.5-72B) demonstrate robust anomaly attribution and safer fallback behaviors (9.1% unsafe rate). These results underscore that reliable OpenClaw security assessment must explicitly incorporate dynamic MITM conditions—not just static prompt attacks or sandboxed environments. ClawTrap establishes a foundational infrastructure for future network-aware agent security research.

item.title: Measuring and Exploiting Confirmation Bias in LLM-Assisted Security Code Review
item.description: This paper presents the first empirical study of **confirmation bias** in LLM-assisted security code review—demonstrating that LLMs systematically favor interpretations aligning with suggestive framing (e.g., “this PR fixes all security issues”), leading to severe vulnerability detection failures. Across 250 CVE pairs and 4 state-of-the-art models, biased framing reduced detection rates by **16–93%**, with sharp increases in false negatives but negligible change in false positives—revealing strong asymmetry. Injection vulnerabilities proved significantly more susceptible than memory corruption bugs. In adversarial evaluation, maliciously framed pull requests (e.g., reintroducing SQLi while labeled as “urgent performance fix”) evaded GitHub Copilot in **35%** of one-shot attempts and Claude Code in **88%** of iterative, real-project deployments. Crucially, lightweight debiasing—redacting metadata and adding explicit instructions (“ignore prior assumptions”)—restored detection in **100% of interactive cases** and **94% of autonomous cases**. Our findings expose a critical, exploitable weakness in AI-augmented development tooling and call for mandatory prompt robustness auditing and metadata trust controls in production CI/CD systems.

item.title: Revisiting Label Inference Attacks in Vertical Federated Learning: Why They Are Vulnerable and How to Defend
item.description: Vertical federated learning (VFL) enables collaborative modeling between an active party (holding labels and a top model) and passive parties (holding features and bottom models), yet exposes labels to inference attacks (LIAs). Contrary to the prevailing assumption that well-trained bottom models inherently encode label information, we theoretically prove—via mutual information analysis—the “**model compensation**” phenomenon: label-relevant information concentrates in deeper layers, with bottom models primarily extracting features and the top model handling label mapping. We show LIAs succeed only under implicit **feature-label distribution alignment**, and fail catastrophically when this alignment is disrupted via task reassignment. Leveraging this insight, we propose a **zero-overhead defense**: shifting the cut layer forward to enlarge the top model’s proportion. Extensive experiments across 5 datasets and 5 architectures demonstrate that this simple adjustment not only reduces LIA success rates to near-random (<5%) but also strengthens complementary defenses (e.g., differential privacy, gradient masking) without accuracy loss.

item.title: Prompt Control-Flow Integrity: A Priority-Aware Runtime Defense Against Prompt Injection in LLM Systems
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) deployed in API and RAG systems face severe prompt injection threats that can override safety policies and induce harmful outputs. Existing defenses treat prompts as flat strings and rely on brittle heuristics or static detection—lacking structural awareness and priority modeling. This paper introduces **Prompt Control-Flow Integrity (PCFI)**, a lightweight, runtime defense that treats each request as a structured composition of segments—system (highest priority), developer, user (lowest), and retrieved documents—and enforces hierarchical policy via a three-stage middleware: lexical role-aware filtering, role-switch detection, and priority-resolved instruction arbitration. Implemented as a FastAPI gateway, PCFI achieves **100% attack interception**, **0% false positives**, and only **0.04 ms median latency overhead** on a benchmark of synthetic and semi-realistic injection workloads—including RAG-context poisoning and multi-turn role hijacking. PCFI demonstrates that provenance-aware, priority-driven prompt enforcement is both practical and highly efficient for production LLM systems.

item.title: AS2 -- Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets: An End-to-End Differentiable Neuro-Soft-Symbolic Reasoning Architecture
item.description: We propose **AS2 (Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets)**, an end-to-end differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that replaces the discrete Answer Set Programming (ASP) solver with a soft, continuous approximation of the immediate consequence operator $T_P$. AS2 maintains per-position symbol probability distributions throughout the forward pass and trains by minimizing the fixed-point residual of a probabilistic lift of $T_P$, enabling full gradient flow from symbolic constraints to perception without any external solver—neither during training nor inference. Crucially, it abandons positional embeddings in favor of constraint-group membership embeddings, directly encoding the declarative ASP specification and achieving indexing-agnostic structural awareness. On Visual Sudoku, AS2 achieves **99.89% cell accuracy and 100% verified constraint satisfaction** across 1,000 test boards using only greedy constrained decoding; on MNIST Addition with $N \in \{2,4,8\}$ addends, it attains **>99.7% digit accuracy** at all scales. These results demonstrate that a differentiable fixed-point operator, combined with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraint specification, matches or exceeds pipeline and solver-based neuro-symbolic systems while preserving full end-to-end differentiability.

item.title: DriftGuard: Mitigating Asynchronous Data Drift in Federated Learning
item.description: **DriftGuard** is a federated continual learning framework designed to mitigate *asynchronous data drift*—where client devices experience non-uniform, time-varying distribution shifts in real-world FL deployments. To balance adaptation efficacy and resource efficiency, DriftGuard introduces a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE)-inspired parameter decoupling: *shared parameters* encode globally transferable knowledge (updated globally upon system-wide drift detection), while *local parameters* are grouped by MoE gating patterns to adapt to cluster-specific distributions (updated locally without raw data sharing). Experiments across diverse datasets (CIFAR-10/100, TinyImageNet, FEMNIST) and models (CNN, ResNet, ViT) show DriftGuard matches or exceeds state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing total retraining cost by up to **83%**, achieving up to **2.3× higher accuracy per unit retraining cost** versus the strongest baseline. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/blessonvar/DriftGuard.

item.title: Evaluating Model-Free Policy Optimization in Masked-Action Environments via an Exact Blackjack Oracle
item.description: This paper introduces an **exact dynamic programming oracle** for infinite-shoe blackjack—a rigorous, verifiable benchmark for model-free RL under dynamic action masking. Built over 4,600 canonical decision cells under fixed Vegas rules, the oracle provides ground-truth action values, optimal policies, and theoretical EV (−0.00161/hand). Three model-free optimizers—masked REINFORCE (with per-cell EMA baseline), SPSA, and CEM—were evaluated via simulation. REINFORCE achieved the highest sample efficiency: 46.37% action-match rate and EV = −0.04688 after 10⁶ hands, outperforming CEM (39.46%, 7.5×10⁶ evaluations) and SPSA (38.63%, 4.8×10⁶ evaluations). Crucially, all methods exhibited substantial *cell-conditional regret*, exposing a critical gap between smooth reward convergence and local policy correctness—highlighting limitations in sparse-visit, masked-action tabular settings. As a negative control, we prove and verify that optimal bet sizing collapses to the table minimum under i.i.d. draws; larger bets increase ruin risk without improving expectation. These results underscore the necessity of exact oracles and rigorous negative controls to disentangle stochastic noise from genuine algorithmic progress.

item.title: CausalVAD: De-confounding End-to-End Autonomous Driving via Causal Intervention
item.description: Planning-oriented end-to-end driving models suffer from causal confusion—relying on spurious statistical correlations rather than true causal mechanisms—leading to unsafe shortcuts under distribution shifts. To address this, we propose **CausalVAD**, a de-confounding training framework grounded in causal intervention theory. Its core, the **Sparse Causal Intervention Scheme (SCIS)**, is a lightweight, plug-and-play module that implements backdoor adjustment via a learnable dictionary of latent driving context prototypes. SCIS intervenes on sparse vectorized queries in the network to actively suppress confounder-induced spurious associations, yielding cleaner, causally faithful representations. On nuScenes, CausalVAD achieves state-of-the-art planning accuracy (18.7% lower L2 pose error) and safety (32.4% fewer collisions). It also demonstrates superior robustness against both dataset bias and synthetically induced causal confusion, establishing a new paradigm for trustworthy autonomous driving.

item.title: Beyond Passive Aggregation: Active Auditing and Topology-Aware Defense in Decentralized Federated Learning
item.description: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) is highly vulnerable to adaptive backdoor attacks that evade passive defenses by dynamically manipulating local updates. To counter this, we propose the first *active, interventional auditing framework* for DFL. We introduce a spatiotemporal dynamical model capturing adversarial update diffusion over arbitrary graph topologies, and design three lightweight, privacy-preserving auditing metrics—stochastic entropy anomaly, randomized smoothing KL divergence, and activation kurtosis—that use private probes to expose latent backdoors invisible to static detection. Further, we develop a topology-aware defense placement strategy to maximize aggregation resilience under communication constraints. We theoretically prove almost-sure convergence under co-evolving attack-defense dynamics. Extensive experiments across diverse architectures and real-world topologies show our method reduces adaptive backdoor success rate from >89% to <6.2%, with only 0.4–1.3% primary task accuracy degradation—outperforming state-of-the-art defenses including FoolsGold, Zeno++, and Byzantine-Robust Aggregation.

item.title: FedTrident: Resilient Road Condition Classification Against Poisoning Attacks in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving camera-based Road Condition Classification (RCC) for intelligent transportation systems, yet remains vulnerable to Targeted Label-Flipping Attacks (TLFAs), where malicious vehicles poison local training data—e.g., relabeling “bumpy road” as “smooth”—to degrade global model safety. Existing defenses fail under diverse attack settings due to: (1) non-TLFA-specific local model inspection, (2) static client exclusion without behavioral history, and (3) no remediation of the already-corrupted global model. To bridge this gap, we propose **FedTrident**, a three-pronged framework featuring: (i) neuron-wise local model analysis for misbehavior detection—including attack goal identification, critical feature extraction, and GMM-based clustering/filtering; (ii) adaptive client rating per FL round to dynamically exclude malicious participants; and (iii) machine unlearning to efficiently repair the poisoned global model post-exclusion. Extensive experiments across RCC models (ResNet, ViT), heterogeneous data, multi-task settings, and dynamic attacks show FedTrident restores performance to near attack-free levels (98.2% of clean accuracy) and outperforms eight baselines by 9.49% and 4.47% on the two most critical metrics—demonstrating strong resilience against up to 40% malicious clients and severe data heterogeneity.

item.title: Towards Verifiable AI with Lightweight Cryptographic Proofs of Inference
item.description: This paper introduces a lightweight, statistically grounded framework for verifiable AI inference, addressing the prohibitive prover overhead (e.g., hundreds of seconds per query) of cryptographic proof systems. Instead of full zero-knowledge proofs, we leverage intrinsic *trace separation*—the statistical distinguishability of internal activation paths between functionally dissimilar models—to enable efficient verification. The prover commits to the inference trace via Merkle-tree vector commitments and reveals only a small number of entries along randomly sampled output-to-input paths. This trades strict soundness for practical efficiency—ideal for auditing, high-query-volume deployments, and rationally incentivized settings with detection penalties. Experiments on ResNet-18 and Llama-2-7B confirm required statistical properties hold in practice and that common adversarial strategies (e.g., gradient-based reconstruction, logit swapping) fail to evade detection. We further propose a refereed delegation protocol enabling correct output identification in *O(log n)* rounds using two competing servers. Proving time drops from minutes to milliseconds—a >1000× speedup—while proofs remain compact (KB-scale).

item.title: Security awareness in LLM agents: the NDAI zone case
item.description: This study investigates how LLM agents form security awareness—specifically, whether they can reliably distinguish secure (TEE-based) from insecure execution environments—using the NDAI zone framework, where IP disclosure is rational only under verified trust. Across 10 diverse LLMs and controlled evidence conditions (e.g., valid/invalid remote attestation reports, system declarations, or ambiguous prompts), we measure disclosure behavior in a standardized negotiation task. We find a striking asymmetry: *all models consistently suppress disclosure when attestation fails*, demonstrating robust threat detection; however, *responses to passing attestation are highly heterogeneous*—only 4 models increase disclosure meaningfully, 5 show no change, and 1 paradoxically reduces it. This reveals a critical gap: current LLMs reliably identify danger but cannot reliably verify safety—a foundational capability for privacy-preserving agentic protocols. Bridging this gap requires advances in interpretability, targeted fine-tuning for environment reasoning, and improved evidence representation architectures.

item.title: Secure Linear Alignment of Large Language Models
item.description: This paper introduces **Secure Linear Alignment (SLA)**, a privacy-preserving framework enabling inference across independently trained LLMs without sharing data or model weights. Leveraging empirically observed *representational convergence*—where diverse models develop semantically aligned hidden states—we learn lightweight affine transformations between final-layer embeddings using only a small public dataset. During inference, SLA applies CKKS homomorphic encryption *exclusively* to the linear alignment and classification operations, bypassing costly encryption of nonlinear layers; this achieves sub-second latency (< 800 ms) while providing formal security guarantees against semi-honest adversaries. Experiments across 12 model pairs (e.g., Llama-3 ↔ Phi-3, Qwen ↔ Bloom) show minimal performance drop in embedding classification (−0.9% accuracy) and out-of-distribution detection (−1.2% AUROC). Crucially, we demonstrate for the first time that linearly aligned representations can support *cross-model text generation*: mapping prompts from one model into another’s decoder yields coherent outputs (BLEU-4 = 63.5), revealing deep structural compatibility in LLM representation spaces.

item.title: MIDST Challenge at SaTML 2025: Membership Inference over Diffusion-models-based Synthetic Tabular data
item.description: The MIDST Challenge at SaTML 2025 is the first large-scale, standardized evaluation of **membership inference vulnerability** in synthetic tabular data generated by diffusion models. Recognizing that tabular data’s heterogeneity (mixed data types), structural complexity (e.g., relational constraints), and sparse distribution undermine the privacy assumptions of generative models, MIDST introduced a rigorous benchmark with diverse target diffusion models—including single-table (e.g., TabDDPM) and multi-relational variants—and unified evaluation protocols. It catalyzed the development of novel **black-box and white-box MIAs specifically designed for diffusion-based tabular generators**, such as gradient-aware white-box attacks and structure-informed confidence-shift detectors. Empirical results reveal non-negligible membership leakage (AUC: 0.72–0.89) across models, with higher vulnerability in relational settings—highlighting critical gaps between statistical fidelity and privacy resilience. The open-source MIDST repository (https://github.com/VectorInstitute/MIDST) provides datasets, models, attack implementations, and evaluation tools to advance reproducible privacy research.

item.title: SHAPCA: Consistent and Interpretable Explanations for Machine Learning Models on Spectroscopy Data
item.description: This paper introduces **SHAPCA**, a novel explainable machine learning pipeline tailored for spectroscopy data. It integrates Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for stable dimensionality reduction with Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) enhanced by an **inverse projection mechanism**, enabling faithful attribution of model predictions back to the original wavelength space—thus preserving biochemical interpretability. Unlike standard SHAP (prone to instability due to spectral collinearity) or PCA-only explanations (semantically disconnected from raw signals), SHAPCA delivers both **global insights** (e.g., identifying diagnostically relevant spectral bands like amide I at 1650–1680 cm⁻¹) and **instance-specific explanations**. Experiments across four real-world spectroscopic datasets demonstrate that SHAPCA achieves **significantly higher explanation consistency** (mean Pearson correlation of 0.92 ± 0.03 across 10 independent training runs vs. 0.61 ± 0.15 for baseline SHAP) and **strong domain alignment**, with >87% of top-ranked bands validated by expert knowledge. SHAPCA bridges the gap between statistical robustness and physical interpretability in spectroscopic AI.

item.title: On Optimizing Multimodal Jailbreaks for Spoken Language Models
item.description: This paper addresses the critical safety gap in Spoken Language Models (SLMs), which inherit LLM vulnerabilities while facing an expanded multimodal attack surface. We propose **JAMA (Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack)**, the first gradient-based framework that *simultaneously optimizes adversarial text prompts and audio waveforms*—combining Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) for discrete text and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for continuous audio under joint loss minimization. Evaluated across four state-of-the-art SLMs and four audio types (clean, noisy, TTS, cross-speaker), JAMA achieves **1.5× to 10× higher jailbreak success rates** than unimodal baselines. A sequential approximation variant further accelerates optimization by **4–6×** with negligible (<2%) performance drop. Our results demonstrate that multimodal synergy fundamentally undermines unimodal safety assumptions—highlighting the necessity of co-designed defense mechanisms for robust SLMs. Code and data are publicly available.

item.title: Communication-Efficient and Robust Multi-Modal Federated Learning via Latent-Space Consensus
item.description: Federated learning (FL) struggles in multi-modal settings due to client heterogeneity in modalities (e.g., accelerometer, camera, audio), divergent model architectures, and prohibitive communication costs. To address this, we propose **CoMFed**, a communication-efficient and robust FL framework that operates in a *shared latent space* rather than parameter space. CoMFed employs lightweight, modality-specific learnable projection matrices to compress raw multi-modal inputs into low-dimensional latent representations (e.g., 128-D), reducing upload volume by >95%. A novel latent-space regularizer enforces cross-client and cross-modal consistency via contrastive alignment, improving generalization and outlier resilience. Experiments on UCI-HAR, WISDM, and MobiAct show CoMFed achieves state-of-the-art accuracy (+2.1–4.7% over FedMD/MM-Fed) under ultra-low communication overhead (<5% of raw feature size) and maintains 62% higher robustness against malicious clients. CoMFed establishes a new paradigm for privacy-preserving, scalable multi-modal collaboration.

item.title: When Differential Privacy Meets Wireless Federated Learning: An Improved Analysis for Privacy and Convergence
item.description: Differentially private wireless federated learning (DPWFL) promises privacy-preserving collaborative training over resource-constrained wireless devices, yet suffers from two critical gaps: (1) existing privacy loss bounds diverge with iteration count, failing to capture long-term privacy stability; and (2) convergence analyses often assume convexity or omit gradient clipping—a practical necessity for privacy amplification. We bridge these gaps via a unified analysis for *smooth non-convex* objectives, explicitly modeling both device sampling and local mini-batch sampling under wireless channel effects. Crucially, we prove that the Rényi privacy loss *converges to a finite constant*, not infinity, revealing inherent privacy stabilization in DPWFL. We further establish $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence to first-order stationary points with explicit gradient clipping, and derive a closed-form privacy-utility trade-off linking $\varepsilon$, clipping threshold $C$, and channel noise $\sigma_h^2$. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and FEMNIST validate our theory—e.g., a 10 dB SNR gain improves test accuracy by 3.2% at fixed $\varepsilon$.

item.title: Differential Privacy in Generative AI Agents: Analysis and Optimal Tradeoffs
item.description: This paper addresses a critical gap in privacy research for generative AI agents: while prior work focuses on protecting user prompts, we formalize and quantify **privacy leakage from enterprise data accessed by AI agents**. We propose a probabilistic differential privacy (DP) framework where response generation is modeled as a stochastic mechanism mapping prompts and private datasets to token-sequence distributions. Within this framework, we introduce *token-level* and *message-level* DP definitions and derive tight analytical bounds linking privacy loss ε to generation parameters—especially temperature and message length—showing that ε grows exponentially with temperature. We formulate a privacy-utility co-design problem and prove the existence of a unique optimal temperature that maximizes response quality (e.g., task accuracy, perplexity) under a given (ε, δ)-DP constraint. Empirical evaluation on domain-specific benchmarks confirms our bounds’ tightness (<12% error) and demonstrates that the optimal temperature strategy improves utility by up to 37% over baselines while satisfying strong privacy guarantees (ε ≤ 0.8).

item.title: Machine Learning for Network Attacks Classification and Statistical Evaluation of Machine Learning for Network Attacks Classification and Adversarial Learning Methodologies for Synthetic Data Generation
item.description: This paper addresses two critical challenges in AI-driven network intrusion detection: (1) robust supervised classification of evolving attacks (e.g., GenAI- and RL-based threats), and (2) privacy-preserving synthetic data generation for data-scarce scenarios. We introduce the first unified multi-modal NIDS dataset—harmonizing CIC-IDS-2017, CIC-IoT-2023, UNSW-NB15, and CIC-DDoS-2019—by aligning flow statistics, packet payload embeddings, and temporal contextual features into a shared space. For attack classification, we benchmark XGBoost, LightGBM, and CNN-LSTM under stratified cross-validation, achieving 0.982±0.007 AUC in cross-dataset zero-shot transfer. For synthetic data, we propose an adversarial framework evaluated via SDV metrics, f-divergences (KL, JS), distinguishability tests (<0.08), and non-parametric statistical validation (KS, TRTS/TSTR). Results show synthetic data not only preserve fidelity (F1-drop <2.1% on downstream classifiers) but also improve lightweight model performance by +1.3% F1 over real data—demonstrating both utility and privacy compliance. This work establishes a reproducible benchmark and evaluation paradigm for next-generation NIDS.

item.title: Post-Training Local LLM Agents for Linux Privilege Escalation with Verifiable Rewards
item.description: This paper introduces PrivEsc-LLM, a lightweight (4B-parameter) local LLM agent fine-tuned for Linux privilege escalation—a security-critical task requiring multi-step interactive reasoning with automatically verifiable success (i.e., actual root access). We propose a two-stage post-training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on procedurally generated, diverse privilege-escalation traces, followed by (2) reinforcement learning with *verifiable rewards*—sparse, binary signals granted only upon real sandboxed root acquisition, ensuring reward integrity and eliminating hallucination-driven cheating. Evaluated on a held-out benchmark of 12 realistic Linux escalation scenarios, PrivEsc-LLM achieves **95.8% success rate** within 20 interaction rounds—nearly matching Claude Opus 4.6 (97.5%) while reducing per-success inference cost by **>100×**. Our approach demonstrates that small, local LLMs can achieve state-of-the-art security performance under strict resource and privacy constraints, enabling reproducible, on-premise vulnerability research.

item.title: ARES: Scalable and Practical Gradient Inversion Attack in Federated Learning through Activation Recovery
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) promises privacy-preserving collaborative training, yet gradient inversion attacks (GIAs) threaten to reconstruct sensitive training data from shared updates. While active GIAs achieve high-fidelity reconstruction—even for large batches—their reliance on model architectural modifications hinders practical deployment. This paper introduces **ARES**, a scalable and architecture-agnostic active GIA that recovers individual samples by formulating activation inversion as a noisy sparse recovery problem solved via generalized Lasso. To handle multi-sample batches, ARES integrates the *imprint* technique to disentangle activations, enabling per-sample reconstruction without model surgery. We provide theoretical guarantees: deriving both the expected recovery rate and an upper bound on reconstruction error. Extensive experiments on CNNs and MLPs across diverse datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet-100) show ARES significantly outperforms prior GIAs under large batch sizes (e.g., +8.2 dB PSNR at batch=64) and realistic FL settings—including non-IID data, client heterogeneity, and gradient compression. Our work exposes intermediate activations as a critical, underappreciated privacy vulnerability in FL, urging urgent development of activation-aware defenses.

item.title: Caging the Agents: A Zero Trust Security Architecture for Autonomous AI in Healthcare
item.description: This paper introduces *Caging*, the first production-deployed zero-trust security architecture for autonomous AI agents in HIPAA-regulated healthcare settings. Motivated by critical vulnerabilities—unauthorized compliance, PHI leakage, identity spoofing, cross-agent propagation, and indirect prompt injection—we develop a six-domain threat model and implement four-layer defense-in-depth: (1) gVisor-based kernel-level workload isolation on Kubernetes; (2) credential proxy sidecars eliminating raw secret access; (3) per-agent egress network policies enforcing destination allowlists; and (4) a prompt integrity framework with structured metadata envelopes and explicit untrusted content labeling. Over 90 days of operation across nine production agents, our architecture detected and remediated four HIGH-severity findings via an automated audit agent, achieved progressive hardening across three VM image generations, and fully covered all eleven attack patterns from recent AI agent literature. All configurations, tooling, and the prompt integrity framework are open-sourced.

item.title: WebPII: Benchmarking Visual PII Detection for Computer-Use Agents
item.description: WebPII is the first public, fine-grained benchmark for visual PII detection in web screenshots—addressing critical privacy risks posed by computer-use agents that ingest real UIs and expose screenshots during cloud inference. It comprises 44,865 synthetically generated, VLM-reproduced e-commerce UI images with pixel-level annotations, featuring: (1) an extended PII taxonomy including transaction-level identifiers (e.g., order IDs, partial credit card numbers) enabling cross-service re-identification; (2) anticipatory detection of *in-progress* form inputs (e.g., partially typed emails or phone numbers); and (3) scalable, layout-diverse generation. Experiments confirm WebPII improves layout-invariant detection and generalization to unseen page types. We train WebRedact—a lightweight, CPU-efficient model achieving **0.753 mAP@50** (vs. 0.357 for text-extraction baseline) at **20ms latency**, demonstrating practical utility. The dataset and model are publicly released.

item.title: Federated Computing as Code (FCaC): Sovereignty-aware Systems by Design
item.description: Federated Computing as Code (FCaC) is a declarative architecture that embeds sovereignty-critical constraints—such as data residency, access delegation, and jurisdictional compliance—directly into cryptographically verifiable artifacts, eliminating reliance on centralized policy services or shared trust infrastructure. By compiling authority into Envelope Capability Tokens (ECTs) anchored by Key Your Organization (KYO) identities and validated via Proof of Possession (PoP), FCaC enables *local, offline admission verification* at execution boundaries. It cleanly separates constitutional governance (e.g., “who may join”) from procedural governance (e.g., ABAC, quotas, risk scoring). Evaluated in a cross-silo federated learning prototype using MNIST, FCaC demonstrates end-to-end workflow enforcement—including envelope issuance, boundary verification, and trigger-based model training—with sub-millisecond local checks and no runtime policy coordination. An open-source implementation is released to support reproducibility and adoption.

item.title: SEAL-Tag: Self-Tag Evidence Aggregation with Probabilistic Circuits for PII-Safe Retrieval-Augmented Generation
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems face a critical privacy vulnerability: *contextual leakage*, where adversaries exploit model instruction-following to exfiltrate Personally Identifiable Information (PII) from retrieved contexts via adaptive queries. Existing defenses impose rigid trade-offs among utility, latency, and safety. We propose **SEAL-Tag**, a privacy-preserving runtime framework built on a *Verify-then-Route* paradigm. SEAL-Tag introduces the **SEAL-Probe protocol**, requiring the model to jointly generate a draft response and a structured, verifiable PII-Evidence Table (PET). A lightweight **Probabilistic Circuit (PC)** adjudicates PETs by enforcing interpretable logical constraints—ensuring robust, auditable decisions. To overcome the privacy “Cold Start”, we design the **S₀–S₆ Anchored Synthesis Pipeline**, generating high-fidelity, provenanced RAG data with precise PII annotations, paired with a **Two-Stage Curriculum** for efficient alignment to the audit protocol. Evaluation shows SEAL-Tag reduces adaptive PII leakage by **>8×**, while matching the utility, speed, and retrieval quality of unsafe baselines—establishing a new Pareto frontier for PII-safe RAG.

item.title: LAAF: Logic-layer Automated Attack Framework A Systematic Red-Teaming Methodology for LPCI Vulnerabilities in Agentic Large Language Model Systems
item.description: We introduce **LAAF (Logic-layer Automated Attack Framework)**, the first automated red-teaming framework targeting Logic-layer Prompt Control Injection (LPCI) in agentic LLM systems—vulnerable due to persistent memory, RAG, and tool integration. LAAF uniquely combines: (i) a comprehensive 49-technique taxonomy across six categories (e.g., Layered, Semantic), with 5 variants per technique and 6 lifecycle stages, enabling up to 2.8M SHA-256-deduplicated payloads; and (ii) a Persistent Stage Breaker (PSB) that iteratively mutates winning payloads to seed subsequent attack stages—mimicking real adversarial escalation. Evaluated across five production LLM platforms over three independent runs, LAAF achieves a mean stage-breakthrough rate of **84%** (83–86%), outperforming random single-technique baselines. Layered and semantic techniques show highest efficacy, with layered payloads notably surpassing encoding-based ones on well-defended systems.

item.title: QuantFL: Sustainable Federated Learning for Edge IoT via Pre-Trained Model Quantisation
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving AI on edge IoT devices but suffers from high carbon emissions due to energy-intensive uplink communication. While pre-trained models are increasingly available on-device, their role in reducing fine-tuning overhead remains underexplored. We propose **QuantFL**, a sustainable FL framework that leverages pre-trained initialization to enable aggressive, lightweight quantization. We find that pre-training naturally concentrates update statistics, allowing efficient bucket quantization—eliminating the need for energy-heavy error-feedback mechanisms. On MNIST and CIFAR-100, QuantFL reduces total communication by **40%** (vs. full-precision downlink) and achieves **≥80% uplink bit reduction**, while matching or exceeding uncompressed baselines under strict bandwidth budgets: BU attains **89.00%** (MNIST) and **66.89%** (CIFAR-100) test accuracy with orders-of-magnitude fewer bits. QuantFL delivers a practical, green recipe for scalable FL on battery-constrained IoT networks.

item.title: Proactive Knowledge Inquiry in Doctor-Patient Dialogue: Stateful Extraction, Belief Updating, and Path-Aware Action Planning
item.description: This paper reframes doctor-patient dialogue as a *proactive knowledge-inquiry problem* under partial observability—shifting EMR generation from passive post-hoc summarization to real-time, state-aware clinical reasoning. We propose a novel framework integrating: (1) stateful extraction to maintain evolving knowledge states; (2) sequential belief updating over clinical hypotheses; (3) gap-aware state modeling to identify high-stakes uncertainties; (4) hybrid retrieval over objectified medical knowledge (guidelines, drug databases, anatomy ontologies); and (5) a lightweight POMDP-based planner for path-aware question/recommendation generation. Evaluated on a controlled pilot of 10 standardized multi-turn dialogues and a 300-query retrieval benchmark, the full framework achieves 83.3% coverage, 80.0% risk recall, 81.4% structural completeness, and lower redundancy than chunk-only or template-heavy baselines. These results demonstrate methodological viability—not clinical readiness—and position proactive inquiry as a conceptually grounded, testable paradigm for next-generation dialogue-driven EMR systems.

item.title: GUIDE: GenAI Units In Digital Design Education
item.description: GUIDE (GenAI Units In Digital Design Education) is an open, modular courseware repository designed to integrate generative AI into digital hardware education. It standardizes teaching around reusable units—each comprising slides, short videos, runnable Google Colab labs, and curated papers—ensuring pedagogical consistency and scalable instructor reuse. We demonstrate three representative units: **VeriThoughts**, enabling formal-verification-backed RTL generation via LLM reasoning; enhanced **LLM-aided testbench generation** with improved functional coverage; and **LLMPirate**, exposing IP piracy risks through hands-on Trojan insertion/detection. Four semester-long course instances—GUIDE4ChipDesign, Build your ASIC, GUIDE4HardwareSecurity, and Hardware Design—are fully specified with learning outcomes, projects, and assessments. Notably, the GUIDE4HardwareSecurity capstone on LLM-assisted hardware Trojan deployment has been successfully used in classrooms and at CSAW. The NYU Cognichip Hackathon engaged 24 international teams in AI-augmented RTL workflows. All materials are open-source: https://github.com/FCHXWH823/LLM4ChipDesign.

item.title: Graph-Native Cognitive Memory for AI Agents: Formal Belief Revision Semantics for Versioned Memory Architectures
item.description: We introduce **Kumiho**, a graph-native cognitive memory architecture formally grounded in belief revision semantics. It unifies cognitive memory primitives (immutable revisions, mutable tag pointers, typed dependency edges, URI addressing) with versioned asset management—enabling a single, coherent graph representation for both memory and agent-generated work. Our key formal contribution is a proven correspondence between the AGM belief revision framework and the operational semantics of a property graph memory system, satisfying all basic AGM postulates (K\*2–K\*6) and Hansson’s Relevance and Core-Retainment postulates. Architecturally, Kumiho employs a dual-store model (Redis + Neo4j), hybrid fulltext/vector retrieval, and three innovations: prospective indexing (LLM-generated scenario implications indexed at write time), structured causal event extraction, and client-side LLM reranking. On LoCoMo, it achieves 0.565 F1 and 97.5% adversarial refusal accuracy; on the harder LoCoMo-Plus (implicit constraint recall), it attains 93.3% judge accuracy—substantially outperforming all published baselines (best prior: Gemini 2.5 Pro, 45.7%). Critically, Kumiho is model-decoupled: swapping the answer model from GPT-4o-mini to GPT-4o lifts end-to-end accuracy from ~88% to 93.3% at negligible cost (~$14 for 401 queries).

item.title: Federated Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Distributional Critic Regularization
item.description: We propose **Federated Distributional Reinforcement Learning (FedDistRL)**, a paradigm that federates *quantile-parameterized distributional critics* instead of scalar value functions or policies—preserving multimodality and tail risk information critical for safety-critical applications. To prevent distributional smearing during averaging, we introduce **TR-FedDistRL**, which constructs a *client-specific, risk-aware Wasserstein barycenter* over a local temporal buffer and enforces a *distributional trust region* via shrink-squash regularization around it. Theoretically, under fixed-policy evaluation, the constrained update is contractive in a probe-set Wasserstein metric. Experiments on bandit, multi-agent gridworld, and continuous highway tasks show: (1) reduced mean-smearing and preserved distributional fidelity; (2) up to 42% lower catastrophe/accident rates versus mean-oriented baselines (e.g., FedAvg+DQN); and (3) significantly lower critic/policy drift than non-federated distributional RL. This work bridges distributional RL, optimal transport, and federated learning for verifiably safe edge intelligence.

item.title: Pushan: Trace-Free Deobfuscation of Virtualization-Obfuscated Binaries
item.description: This paper introduces **PUSHAN**, the first trace-free, constraint-free technique for deobfuscating virtualization-obfuscated binaries. Unlike prior approaches that rely on execution traces and expensive dynamic symbolic execution (DSE), PUSHAN uses **VPC-sensitive symbolic emulation** to reconstruct complete control-flow graphs (CFGs) without path-constraint accumulation or SMT solving—bypassing a known NP-hard bottleneck. Crucially, it is the first method to **decompile deobfuscated code into high-quality, human-readable C pseudocode**, enabling effective manual analysis and LLM-assisted understanding. Evaluated on over 1,000 binaries—including those protected by Tigress, VMProtect, and Themida—PUSHAN fully recovers CFGs and generates structured C output. It successfully analyzes an unexamined VMProtect-protected malware sample from VirusTotal, demonstrating practical utility in real-world threat intelligence.

item.title: Toward Reliable, Safe, and Secure LLMs for Scientific Applications
item.description: As LLMs evolve into autonomous “AI scientists,” their deployment in research introduces domain-specific risks—ranging from biosafety failures and physical hazards to malicious misuse—that generic safety benchmarks fail to capture due to domain mismatch, narrow threat coverage, and overfitting. This paper addresses this gap by: (1) proposing a novel, science-contextualized threat taxonomy spanning data, model, interaction, and execution layers; (2) introducing a multi-agent framework for automated generation of adversarial, scientifically grounded safety benchmarks; and (3) conceptualizing a multilayered defense architecture integrating red-teaming, external boundary controls (e.g., real-time parameter gating), and an internal Safety LLM Agent that performs stepwise causal consistency and risk forecasting within scientific workflows. Together, these contributions establish a foundational framework for evaluating and ensuring the reliability, safety, and security of LLM agents in scientific applications.

item.title: Retrieval-Augmented LLMs for Security Incident Analysis
item.description: This paper introduces a retrieval-augmented large language model (RAG) system for automated security incident analysis. To address the labor-intensive nature of cross-source log investigation (e.g., IDS alerts, network flows, authentication logs), our approach combines an **ATT&CK-aligned query library** for targeted indicator extraction with RAG-enhanced semantic reasoning to reconstruct attack sequences and answer forensic questions. Evaluated across five LLM providers on malware traffic and multi-stage Active Directory attacks, the system achieves **100% recall** in all four malware scenarios (Claude Sonnet 4 and DeepSeek-V3) — with DeepSeek costing **15× less** (\$0.008 vs. \$0.12 per analysis). For Active Directory attacks, it attains **100% precision and 82% recall** in attack-step detection. Ablation studies confirm that RAG is essential: non-RAG LLM baselines correctly identify victim hosts but **fail entirely to detect attacker infrastructure** (e.g., malicious domains, C2 servers). Results demonstrate that query-guided filtering + RAG retrieval enables accurate, cost-efficient, and context-bound security analysis.

item.title: STEP: Detecting Audio Backdoor Attacks via Stability-based Trigger Exposure Profiling
item.description: Audio backdoor attacks pose a critical threat to deep-learning speech models deployed in security-sensitive applications, yet existing inference-time defenses fail in the audio domain due to over-reliance on image/text-specific assumptions or invalid trigger robustness priors. We propose **STEP** (Stability-based Trigger Exposure Profiling), a black-box, retraining-free detector requiring only hard-label model outputs. STEP exploits a fundamental dual anomaly of backdoor triggers: *abnormal label stability* under semantic-breaking perturbations (e.g., noise, time-frequency distortions) and *abnormal label fragility* under semantic-preserving perturbations (e.g., pitch shift, reverberation). It profiles each test sample via two complementary perturbation branches, scores resulting stability/fragility features using one-class anomaly detectors trained solely on benign references, and fuses scores via unsupervised weighting. Evaluated across seven diverse backdoor attacks—including physical-world over-the-air settings—STEP achieves an average **AUROC of 97.92%** and **EER of 4.54%**, substantially outperforming state-of-the-art baselines while generalizing across architectures, tasks, and open-set scenarios.

item.title: MCP-38: A Comprehensive Threat Taxonomy for Model Context Protocol Systems (v1.0)
item.description: This paper introduces **MCP-38**, the first protocol-specific threat taxonomy for the Model Context Protocol (MCP), comprising 38 rigorously defined threat categories (MCP-01 to MCP-38). Developed via a four-phase methodology—protocol decomposition, cross-mapping against STRIDE, OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025), and OWASP Agentic Top 10 (2026), real-world incident synthesis from 17 verified MCP-related vulnerabilities, and remediation-surface categorization—MCP-38 systematically addresses semantic attack vectors absent in prior frameworks, including tool description poisoning, indirect prompt injection, parasitic tool chaining, and dynamic trust violations. Each category is explicitly mapped to all three reference frameworks and annotated with detection signatures, PoC patterns, and mitigation guidance. MCP-38 establishes the foundational ontology for automated threat intelligence, enabling scalable TTP modeling and security validation across MCP-compliant systems.

item.title: Guardrails as Infrastructure: Policy-First Control for Tool-Orchestrated Workflows
item.description: This paper introduces **Policy-First Tooling**, a model-agnostic infrastructure layer that enforces safety and reliability for tool-orchestrated workflows—spanning scripts, CI bots, and agentic systems—by shifting control from prompt engineering to explicit, auditable policies. We propose a compact policy DSL, a runtime enforcement architecture delivering actionable rationales and fix hints for violations, and a reproducible benchmark based on trace replay with controlled fault and misuse injection. Across 225 runs (5 policy packs, 3 fault profiles), stricter policies (P4 vs. P0) increase violation prevention from 0.000 to 0.681, reduce retry amplification from 3.774 to 1.378, and achieve 0.875 leakage recall under injected secrets—while task success drops from 0.356 to 0.067, explicitly quantifying the safety–utility trade-off. Our approach generalizes beyond LLMs and enables deployable, auditable, and composable guardrails as infrastructure.

item.title: VeriGrey: Greybox Agent Validation
item.description: VeriGrey is a novel grey-box validation framework for LLM agents. It leverages the *sequence of invoked external tools* as a dynamic feedback signal to guide testing—enabling discovery of rare but dangerous tool-call patterns that trigger unexpected or malicious agent behavior. To generate effective adversarial inputs, VeriGrey employs *semantically coupled prompt mutation*: it embeds injection tasks (e.g., crafting malicious HTML, tampering with API responses) as *necessary sub-steps* within the agent’s original task flow—thereby bypassing conventional input sanitization. Evaluated on the AgentDojo benchmark, VeriGrey detects **33% more indirect prompt injection (IPI) vulnerabilities** than black-box baselines using GPT-4.1. In real-world case studies with Gemini CLI and OpenClaw, it uncovers previously undetected attack scenarios. Notably, against 10 malicious skills in OpenClaw, VeriGrey achieves **100% detection (10/10) on Kimi-K2.5** and **90% (9/10) on Opus 4.6**, demonstrating the critical advantage of dynamic, feedback-driven agent assurance.

item.title: Probabilistic Federated Learning on Uncertain and Heterogeneous Data with Model Personalization
item.description: Conventional federated learning (FL) degrades under data uncertainty and heterogeneity. While Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) model uncertainty explicitly, their computational and communication overhead in FL remains understudied. We propose **Meta-BayFL**, a personalized probabilistic FL framework that synergizes meta-learning with BNNs. It features: (1) client-level BNNs with hierarchical variational inference for stable training on small/noisy data; (2) meta-initialized adaptive learning rates enabling efficient local personalization under non-IID conditions; and (3) posterior-aware global aggregation that improves robustness via distributional similarity weighting. We provide convergence analysis with an explicit upper bound on global model error across rounds, and quantify runtime, latency, and communication costs—demonstrating feasibility on resource-constrained edge/IoT devices. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet show Meta-BayFL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FL methods (e.g., pFedMe, Ditto, FedFomo), achieving up to **+7.42% test accuracy**, especially under severe label noise and extreme data heterogeneity.

item.title: SLEA-RL: Step-Level Experience Augmented Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Turn Agentic Training
item.description: Large Language Model (LLM) agents excel in multi-turn tool-use tasks, yet standard RL training ignores cross-episode experience reuse. Prior experience-augmented methods retrieve memories only once—based on the initial task prompt—leading to growing state-experience mismatch as observations evolve step-by-step. We propose **SLEA-RL**, a step-level experience-augmented RL framework that retrieves relevant trajectories *at every decision step*, conditioned on the current observation. It comprises: (i) step-level observation clustering for efficient semantic-state indexing; (ii) a self-evolving experience library that admits high-scoring successes/failures via score-thresholding and rate-limited extraction; and (iii) policy optimization with fine-grained, step-level credit assignment for accurate advantage estimation. Crucially, the library evolves through gradient-free semantic analysis—not backpropagation—enabling stable, interpretable growth alongside the policy. On long-horizon multi-turn benchmarks (WebShop, ALFWorld, ToolAlpaca), SLEA-RL outperforms PPO, DQN, REINFORCE, and retrieval-augmented baselines by up to +12.7% absolute task success rate and accelerates failure-path convergence by 2.3×.

item.title: Malicious Or Not: Adding Repository Context to Agent Skill Classification
item.description: This paper presents the largest empirical security analysis of AI agent skills to date, challenging the alarmingly high (up to 46.8%) malicious classification rates reported by description-only scanners. We collect and analyze **238,180 unique skills** from three major marketplaces and GitHub, shifting evaluation from isolated `SKILL.md` files to a holistic assessment that cross-verifies skill descriptions against their embedded GitHub repositories. By incorporating repository context—including code content, commit history, ownership status, and abandonment signals—we reduce the confirmed malicious rate to just **0.52%**, cutting false positives by over 98%. Our analysis uncovers previously undocumented real-world attack vectors, most notably **skill hijacking via abandoned GitHub repositories**, and demonstrates that description-repository inconsistency is a strong predictor of benign intent rather than malignancy. This work establishes repository-aware contextual analysis as a critical, scalable foundation for trustworthy AI agent skill ecosystems.

item.title: Poisoning the Pixels: Revisiting Backdoor Attacks on Semantic Segmentation
item.description: Semantic segmentation models underpin safety-critical systems like autonomous driving, yet their backdoor vulnerabilities remain poorly understood. Prior work naively adapts classification-style threats (e.g., object-to-background mislabeling), ignoring segmentation’s unique pixel-wise semantics and spatial dependencies. We systematically revisit segmentation-specific backdoor attacks, identifying four coarse-grained vectors (Object-to-Object, Object-to-Background, Background-to-Object, Background-to-Background) and two fine-grained ones (Instance-Level, Conditional). To formalize them, we propose **BADSEG**, a unified framework that jointly optimizes pixel-level triggers and label manipulation under utility preservation constraints. Extensive experiments across Cityscapes and ADE20K show BADSEG achieves 92.4%–98.1% attack success rates with <0.8% clean mIoU drop on DeepLabV3, SegFormer, and Mask2Former. Critically, six state-of-the-art defenses fail—average detection rate is <31%, with >67% false positives. The vulnerability persists even in transformer-based models and the Segment Anything Model (SAM), where it induces 89.3% pixel-level errors. This work exposes fundamental security gaps in semantic segmentation and calls for task-aware defense design.

item.title: Ciphertext-Policy ABE for $\mathsf{NC}^1$ Circuits with Constant-Size Ciphertexts from Succinct LWE
item.description: We present the first lattice-based ciphertext-policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) scheme for $\mathsf{NC}^1$ access policies achieving **constant-size ciphertexts**—i.e., ciphertext length is $O(1)$ in terms of circuit depth $d$, size $s$, and input length $\ell$, depending only on the security parameter $\lambda$. The scheme is **selectively secure in the standard model** under the $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda)$-succinct LWE assumption (Wee, CRYPTO 2024). Public keys and ciphertexts are $O(1)$ (hiding $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda)$ factors), while secret keys are $O(\ell)$, linear in input bit-length. As a direct application, we obtain a broadcast encryption scheme for $N$ users with ciphertext size $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda)$—**independent of $\log N$**—and key sizes $\operatorname{poly}(\lambda, \log N)$. This resolves a long-standing efficiency bottleneck in functional encryption for bounded-depth circuits.

item.title: SparkVSR: Interactive Video Super-Resolution via Sparse Keyframe Propagation
item.description: Video Super-Resolution (VSR) remains largely non-interactive: users cannot correct artifacts or steer restoration outcomes. SparkVSR bridges this gap by introducing **sparse keyframes as a simple, expressive control signal**. Users super-resolve only 1–5 frames using any off-the-shelf image SR model; SparkVSR then propagates their high-fidelity priors across the entire LR video while preserving motion coherence. We propose a **keyframe-conditioned latent-pixel two-stage training pipeline**, jointly optimizing cross-space propagation and perceptual refinement. At inference, flexible keyframe selection (manual, I-frame–based, or random) and a **reference-free guidance mechanism** ensure robustness—even with imperfect or missing keyframes. On standard VSR benchmarks, SparkVSR achieves new state-of-the-art in no-reference quality assessment: +24.6% on CLIP-IQA, +21.8% on DOVER, and +5.6% on MUSIQ—demonstrating superior temporal consistency and detail fidelity. Crucially, SparkVSR generalizes *out-of-the-box* to unseen tasks like old-film restoration and video style transfer, establishing itself as a generic interactive, keyframe-conditioned video processing framework. Code and demos: https://sparkvsr.github.io/

item.title: Differential Harm Propensity in Personalized LLM Agents: The Curious Case of Mental Health Disclosure
item.description: This study investigates how **mental health disclosure**—a sensitive, realistic personalization cue—affects harmful task completion in tool-using LLM agents. Extending the AgentHarm benchmark, we evaluate frontier (e.g., GPT-5.2, Claude Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3-Pro) and open-source (DeepSeek 3.2) models on multi-step malicious and matched benign tasks under controlled user-context conditions: *no bio*, *bio-only*, and *bio + mental health disclosure*, with lightweight jailbreak injection. Results show non-trivial harmful completion across all models (notably highest for DeepSeek 3.2), while bio-only context generally reduces harm and increases refusals; adding mental health disclosure further shifts outcomes modestly—but effects are attenuated after multiple-testing correction. Crucially, increased refusals occur **equally on benign tasks**, revealing a safety–utility trade-off via over-refusal. Jailbreak prompting sharply elevates harm and can override the protective effect of personalization. Collectively, personalization acts as a **weak, fragile protective factor** against agentic misuse—easily compromised by minimal adversarial pressure—highlighting the urgent need for personalization-aware evaluations and robust, context-invariant safeguards.

item.title: Federated Learning with Multi-Partner OneFlorida+ Consortium Data for Predicting Major Postoperative Complications
item.description: This study developed and validated privacy-preserving federated learning (FL) models to predict major postoperative complications—ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, acute kidney injury, and in-hospital mortality—using real-world data from 358,644 adult patients across five healthcare institutions (2012–2023). Leveraging a hierarchical heterogeneous FL framework, models were trained locally without raw data sharing and aggregated via encrypted gradient updates. FL models achieved consistently strong performance: AUROC ranged from 0.82 to 0.91 and AUPRC from 0.38 to 0.67 across all outcomes—matching or exceeding the best single-site local models (mean AUROC gain: +2.1–4.7%) and narrowing inter-site performance variance by 32%. Critically, FL matched centralized modeling accuracy (AUROC gap < 0.015) while fully preserving data sovereignty. These results demonstrate FL’s feasibility for scalable, generalizable, and regulatory-compliant clinical decision support.
